Primary customers: Mass-market consumers, sold via SodaStream's own retail/e-commerce channels and third-party retailers (supermarkets, department stores, e-commerce) in 40+ countries.
Year founded: 1903 (as a UK carbonation-apparatus business); modern Israeli parent (Soda-Club) founded 1991 — see note below
Ownership status: Subsidiary of PepsiCo, Inc. (acquired August 2018)
Core model: SodaStream sells a B2C razor-and-blades model: consumers buy a home carbonation machine and reusable CO2 cylinders, then return empty cylinders for exchange (via SodaStream's own retail/online exchange program or third-party retail exchange points) rather than buying new ones — each returned cylinder is inspected, sterilized and refilled through what the company calls its "Triple Quality Control" process before re-entering circulation. This cylinder-exchange loop is the company's core reuse mechanism and displaces both single-use beverage packaging (bottles/cans) and single-use gas canisters.

Primary customers: B2B: independent restaurants, corporate and institutional canteens, catering companies (e.g., Aramark, Compass Group), delivery platforms, retail/convenience chains, event and stadium operators, and municipalities. B2C: individual consumers who borrow containers when ordering takeaway or delivery food from partner venues.
Ownership status: Private, venture-backed
Core model: Vytal runs a B2B software-and-hardware platform: it supplies reusable containers to food-service partners (independent restaurants, canteens, corporate catering, delivery platforms, stadiums and event operators), who pay Vytal a per-use fee for each container issued. Categories: Packaging Solutions — Reusable Packaging / Deposit-Free Reuse Platform
Key partners / customers: Named partners reported in company and press materials include Uber Eats, Lieferando, KFC, PepsiCo, Aramark, Compass Group, Valora, Center Parcs, and event/media partners including UEFA Euro 2024 Fanzones, Hyrox, and Live Nation's Insomniac Festival Group. The company states it has more than 7,000 partner locations across 20+ countries (self-reported, current figure not independently audited).

No independently audited tonnage of plastic or CO2 avoided was found in this research pass.

Primary customers: Individual consumers in the UK (primary market) and increasingly other geographies, purchasing via direct-to-consumer subscription (wearewild.com) and through major UK supermarket and health/beauty retail chains.
Year founded: Company incorporated 24 December 2018; founders describe the brand as established 2019; Unilever's own acquisition announcement states Wild "launched in the UK in 2020"
Ownership status: Subsidiary of Unilever plc (acquired 1 April 2025) — previously an independent, venture-backed private limited company co-founded and majority-owned by Charlie Bowes-Lyon and Freddy Ward
Core model: Wild's core model is refill-based personal care: customers buy a durable, reusable case (deodorant, and later body wash, hand wash and lip balm formats) and then purchase compostable or recyclable refill inserts rather than a new plastic container each time. Sales channels include a direct-to-consumer subscription/e-commerce model via wearewild.com and broad UK retail distribution — the brand is stocked in Tesco, Sainsbury's, Waitrose, Boots, Superdrug and Selfridges, reportedly reaching over 10,000 stores. Short summary: Wild is a UK personal-care brand built around refillable, plastic-reduced products, starting with a natural deodorant sold as a reusable metal case plus compostable/bamboo-pulp refill sticks. It was co-founded by childhood friends Charlie Bowes-Lyon and Freddy Ward, with the operating company (Wild Cosmetics Limited) incorporated in December 2018 and the brand launching commercially in the UK in 2019/2020. Wild grew rapidly through direct-to-consumer subscription and expansion into major UK retailers, reporting revenue of £46.9 million
